rows.title: Vietnam 15th National Assembly Passes Law on Artificial Intelligence, Effective March 2026
rows.summary:
Vietnam's 15th National Assembly has passed the Law on Artificial Intelligence, establishing a comprehensive statutory framework governing AI development, deployment and oversight across the country, with the law scheduled to enter into force on 1 March 2026.

On 11 December 2025, the 15th National Assembly of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam approved the Law on Artificial Intelligence, presented to the legislature by Minister of Science and Technology Nguyen Manh Hung. The instrument comprises 35 articles and makes Vietnam one of a small number of countries to have enacted a dedicated, comprehensive legislative framework for artificial intelligence.

The Law articulates a human-centred principle, providing that AI must serve human interests rather than replace human agency, and stipulating that human oversight is required in important decision-making processes. The instrument establishes the legal foundation for domestic AI autonomy across computing infrastructure, data governance and research capacity. In support of these objectives, the State commits to investing in a national AI computing centre and a controlled open-data system, with the legislators determining that these measures will reduce computing costs, lower barriers to market entry and promote a competitive and transparent AI ecosystem. The Law further establishes a national AI development fund and introduces an AI Voucher mechanism to assist businesses in adopting AI technologies.

The Law creates a controlled experimental sandbox regime for sensitive AI applications, under which certain legal liabilities are waived to enable technology enterprises, particularly high-technology startups, to test sensitive AI solutions at reduced risk and cost. The instrument also addresses AI-generated content, algorithmic ethics and the responsibilities of cross-border AI service platform operators, extending regulatory scope to international service providers active in the Vietnamese market. The legislators stated that these provisions are designed to align Vietnam with international standards while preserving digital sovereignty.

rows.title: Vietnam National Assembly Passes New Law on Cybersecurity, Effective July 2026
rows.summary:
Vietnam's National Assembly has passed a new Law on Cybersecurity, consolidating two predecessor statutes into a single instrument comprising eight chapters and 45 articles, with the Ministry of Public Security designated as the lead coordinating authority.

On 10 December 2025, the National Assembly of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam passed the Law on Cybersecurity, with 434 of the 443 deputies present voting in favour. The law will take effect from 1 July 2026. No financial penalties are specified in the source material.

Presenting the report on the draft law, Minister of Public Security General Luong Tam Quang stated that the Ministry of Public Security, as the drafting body, developed the 2025 Law on Cybersecurity by merging the 2018 Law on Cybersecurity and the 2015 Law on Cyberinformation Security. The Minister noted that the consolidation does not alter the functions or responsibilities of existing ministries and sectors, nor does it introduce new policies. The Ministry further reviewed and incorporated relevant provisions of the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime, known as the Hanoi Convention, into the domestic legislative framework, adding provisions on international cooperation in combating cybercrime and on cybersecurity protection measures, in response to recommendations from National Assembly deputies.

Under the Government's assignment, the Ministry of Public Security is designated as the standing agency coordinating the National Cybersecurity Incident Response Alliance, which comprises relevant authorities and enterprises. According to the source, the Ministry's role is to ensure swift and effective response to cybersecurity incidents and threats to information systems of ministries, sectors, localities and key enterprises. The Ministry is also required to ensure the collection of data and electronic evidence in accordance with the Criminal Procedure Code to support the investigation and handling of cybercrime. Minister Luong Tam Quang characterised ensuring cybersecurity as a critical, regular, urgent and strategic task, describing it as a responsibility shared by the entire political system and the Vietnamese people.
